title: Polymarket’s Corporate Structure Is a Mystery—Even to Some of Its Former Employees
gist: Polymarket's Panama-based entity Adventure One QSS, created to satisfy a 2022 CFTC settlement, appears to have been staffed largely from New York rather than Panama, raising questions about whether the offshore structure was ever real even as the CFTC's posture toward the company has grown friendlier.

themes.theme: Panama shell with no Panama staff

insights.claim: Polymarket's Panama-incorporated entity Adventure One QSS, set up to satisfy a 2022 CFTC settlement, appears to have been staffed largely by employees working from New York rather than Panama, according to former employees interviewed by WIRED.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Suggests the offshore compliance structure may be nominal rather than operational, creating latent regulatory exposure for Polymarket/Blockratize.
insights.locator: Despite this structure, WIRED found that Adventure One QSS had staff working out of the US.

insights.claim: Former Adventure One QSS employees say they never traveled to Panama and never reported to or interacted with Panama-based colleagues, because, in their account, no colleagues were actually located there.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Direct testimony contradicts the entity's formal offshore staffing claim, a fact pattern regulators could revisit.
insights.quote: because, they say, there were no colleagues there
insights.locator: These employees did not travel to Panama, report to anyone in Panama

insights.claim: NPR's earlier reporting corroborates WIRED's findings, having found that Adventure One QSS's headquarters in a Panama City skyscraper was empty and that the company had no Panama-based staff.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Two independent outlets converging on the same finding raises the credibility that the offshore structure is a facade rather than an isolated anomaly.
insights.locator: WIRED's reporting aligns with previous coverage from NPR

insights.claim: A former Polymarket staffer said New York-based workers who wrote code, set up event contracts, or otherwise ran the offshore platform were technically employed by Adventure One QSS, but that there was no practical barrier separating the two corporate entities day to day.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Blurred operational lines between the offshore and onshore entities undercut the legal separation the CFTC settlement was designed to enforce.
insights.locator: One former Polymarket staffer said that the workers in New York who "touched code"

insights.claim: The 2022 CFTC settlement fined Blockratize, the corporate entity associated with Polymarket, $1.4 million and ordered it to wind down markets that violated the Commodity Exchange Act.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Establishes the regulatory baseline against which Adventure One QSS's actual staffing location must be judged for compliance.

insights.claim: According to Joseph Konizeski, a former CFTC chief trial attorney, complying with the 2022 settlement required Polymarket to hire new staff offshore, move corporate infrastructure offshore, and stop accepting funds from US customers.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Defines the specific operational bar Adventure One QSS needed to clear; WIRED's reporting suggests it was not met.
insights.quote: That would have required them to hire new staff offshore, move their corporate infrastructure offshore, and stop accepting funds from US customers.
insights.locator: According to Joseph Konizeski, former chief trial attorney

insights.claim: A former CFTC lawyer says the true location of Adventure One QSS's staff is directly relevant to whether Polymarket actually satisfied the settlement's terms.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Signals a plausible reopening angle for enforcement if regulators choose to scrutinize settlement compliance rather than only marketing conduct.
insights.quote: We would have really liked to know that the people purportedly in Panama weren’t actually in Panama
insights.locator: Former CFTC employees see the location of Adventure One's staff as relevant

insights.claim: Adventure One QSS's 2021 incorporation papers named Panama-based officers, including resident agent Mario Ernesto García de Paredes and initial company president Diana Munoz, but Munoz was replaced within two months by Polymarket CEO Shayne Coplan, who is based in New York.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: The swift replacement of the Panama-based president with the New York-based CEO is a concrete governance signal that the Panama presence was thin from the start.
insights.locator: As Sportico reported, Adventure One QSS did initially name Panama residents

insights.claim: Omar Camargo, listed as Adventure One QSS's secretary, and Diana Munoz appear to have worked together previously, both listed as executives in securities filings tied to a separate company, Internet Art Foundation.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: A recurring pair of nominal officers across unrelated entities is a pattern worth flagging when assessing how substantive the Panama incorporation was.
insights.locator: Munoz and Camargo appear to have worked together before

insights.claim: NYSE parent Intercontinental Exchange's announced investment of up to $2 billion into Polymarket actually flows into Blockratize only, not into Adventure One QSS, meaning the offshore entity running much of the platform sits outside that capital and any associated oversight.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Investors and partners tied to the ICE investment have no direct claim on or visibility into the offshore Adventure One entity, a structural risk worth tracking.
insights.locator: New York Stock Exchange parent company Intercontinental Exchange has announced

insights.claim: Inside Polymarket, the larger Blockratize team focused on the newer US-licensed Polymarket US platform and marketing, while a smaller group assigned to Adventure One QSS handled offshore-platform tasks without permanent desks, leaving employees unsure why the workplace was split this way.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Internal confusion about the org split suggests the dual-entity structure was designed more for legal form than operational substance.
insights.locator: A former employees described a workplace in which Adventure One team members

insights.claim: The CFTC dropped its investigation into Polymarket in July 2025 without bringing charges, following a May 2025 staff letter that changed the agency's approach to foreign futures and cross-border swap rules.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Marks a policy inflection toward leniency for offshore-structured crypto and prediction-market platforms under the current administration.
insights.locator: The CFTC dropped an investigation into Polymarket in July 2025

insights.claim: President Trump pardoned Binance founder Changpeng Zhao in 2025, after Zhao and Binance had pleaded guilty in a 2023 case alleging Commodity Exchange Act violations and inadequate anti-money-laundering controls.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Illustrates a broader normalization of past crypto-regulatory offenses that likely factors into how aggressively Polymarket's own structure will be pursued.
insights.locator: President Donald Trump pardoned Zhao in 2025

insights.claim: Former CFTC enforcement attorney Jack Murphy says the agency under the current administration has deprioritized cases centered on simply running an unlicensed exchange, instead focusing on intentional misconduct and retail-trader protection.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Implies Adventure One QSS's offshore-structure question is less likely to trigger enforcement than deceptive marketing conduct would.
insights.quote: The CFTC’s Enforcement Division is prioritizing investigations involving intentional misconduct and is focused on protecting retail traders.
insights.locator: According to former CFTC enforcement trial attorney Jack Murphy

themes.theme: New probe driven by marketing fraud, not structure

insights.claim: A Wall Street Journal investigation found Polymarket built copies of its own website for influencer partners to stage fake winning bets, prompting significant backlash and calls for a federal probe; Polymarket has said it will audit its active promotional content.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: A distinct reputational and regulatory risk vector from the offshore-structure question; the outcome of Polymarket's promised audit is worth tracking.
insights.locator: A recent Wall Street Journal investigation into Polymarket's affiliate marketing practices

insights.claim: Sources confirmed to WIRED that a CFTC investigation into Polymarket, first reported by the Wall Street Journal, is ongoing, and it appears to concern marketing practices rather than the Adventure One QSS staffing question.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Two live regulatory threads exist against Polymarket simultaneously: settlement-compliance risk (unaddressed) and marketing-fraud risk (active investigation).
insights.locator: Sources familiar with the matter confirmed to WIRED that the investigation is ongoing

insights.claim: The CFTC has previously pursued firms for falsely claiming offshore operations: a 2021 complaint alleged forex company WorldWideMarkets was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands but actually run from a New Jersey office, a case reportedly nearing settlement.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Direct precedent for how the CFTC could treat Adventure One QSS's US-based staffing if it chooses to revisit the structural question.
insights.locator: According to a 2021 complaint, a foreign exchange company called WorldWideMarkets

insights.claim: Financial-services regulation expert Todd Phillips describes Polymarket's Panama/New York offshore arrangement as odd even if it is legal, framing WIRED's findings as sitting in a regulatory gray zone rather than a clear violation.
insights.speaker: Kate Knibbs
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations of imminent enforcement: the arrangement may be unusual without being unlawful.
insights.locator: Todd Phillips, a financial services regulation expert, describes the setup as odd
